data.title: Super Micro Secures $700M in Debt--Smart Move or Risky Bet?
data.description:
Feb 21 - Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI, Financial) performs a strategic move by issuing $700 million in convertible senior notes. Super Micro Computer finalized the 2.25% convertible senior notes due 2028 with unsecured maturity on July 15, 2028, though the notes become redeemable before term through redemption or conversion or sukuk repurchase. Interest payments for the notes will begin in the first half of July 2025. Super Micro Computer Inc. intends to allocate the funds generated from its $700 million note issuance for corporate expenses, which include building operational working capital to sustain future growth initiatives.

The notes provide noteholders with the option of redemption following March 1, 2026, according to the indenture provisions, while a fundamental change in the company entitles them to demand a repurchase. The debt-to-equity rate equals 16.3784 shares for every $1000 of the original principal and suggests each share holds a price of $61.06 yet.

data.title: Swiss Stocks End Week Upbeat; Sika Shares Up
data.description:
Swiss stocks ended Friday in the green, with the Swiss Market Index closing 1.10% higher amid another busy earnings day.

Companies that reported financial results included Swiss specialty chemicals group Sika , lender Liechtensteinische Landesbank , biotechnology investor BB Biotech and real estate developer Allreal .

Sika gained 0.85% after it delivered record full-year 2024 results, logging a year-over-year increase in net sales to 11.76 billion francs from 11.24 billion francs and a 17.4% jump in net profit to 1.25 billion francs, with all regions contributing positively to the results. Looking ahead, the company projects a 3% to 6% sales growth in local currencies for 2025.

Despite Sika's slower-than-expected recovery due to headwinds from China and the automotive sector, analysts at Baader Helvea expect the company's growth to continue in 2025 as it capitalizes on its diversified market presence, particularly focusing on infrastructure and refurbishment. "Moreover, Sika will not be impacted by U.S. tariffs, as its value chain for the U.S. market is located domestically, and it is well-positioned to benefit from reconstruction efforts in Ukraine. Overall, we see the current entry point into this long-term success story, with an annual EPS growth potential of over 10%, as extremely attractive," they added.

Meanwhile, Swiss building materials company Holcim's North American business will be rebranded as Amrize following its planned spin-off, which is anticipated to complete by the end of the first half. Amrize is intended to be listed on the New York Stock Exchange and the SIX Swiss Exchange. Holcim's shares were down 0.49% at closing.

Elsewhere and in economic news, the HCOB Eurozone Composite PMI Output Index came in at 50.2 in February, unchanged from January, flash data from S&P Global and Hamburg Commercial Bank showed. The latest reading indicates a sustained but marginal private sector growth, with Germany seeing an increase in output.

"In February, the PMI again flagged rising input costs that are being priced through to the consumer to some extent. Then again, it's a stretch to expect a marked flare up of medium-term inflation in the current weak demand environment. We expect the ECB to continue to lower rates for the time being," ING said.

data.title: National Bank Cuts TVA Group's Price Target on Q4 'Underperformance'
data.description:
National Bank of Canada cut TVA Group's (TVA-B.TO) price target to $1.00 from $1.25 and maintained a sector-perform rating in a note on Friday after the company reported fourth quarter results on Thursday.

Net loss attributable to the shareholders narrowed to $1.1 million in the fourth quarter from a year-ago figure of $15.9 million. Quarterly loss per share was $0.03 versus $0.37, in the prior year period.

Revenue for the three months ended Dec. 31, decreased to $146.7 million compared with $151.7 million, a year earlier.

National Bank analyst Adam Shine said TVA's EBITDA of $5 million was below National Bank's forecast of $11.3 million due to weaker profits from the Broadcasting and Film Production and Audiovisual services segment.

The bank pushed out TVA's valuation and cut the company's target due to "underperformance."

data.title: GBPUSD falls away from 100 day MA after breaks above fail. Buyers had their shot & missed.
data.description:
The GBPUSD spent the first half of the week trading choppily around the 38.2% retracement of the September high to January low at 1.26076, reflecting market indecision.

On Wednesday, after a move lower, buyers stepped in just ahead of the 1.25499 swing high from two weeks ago, pushing the pair back above the 38.2% retracement and toward the 100-day moving average (1.2660, blue line). However, similar to the retracement level, price action around the 100-day MA was indecisive, fluctuating above and below in the Asian-Pacific session before mostly trading below in the North American morning session. Buyers had an opportunity to take control—but failed.

Key Technical Levels Going Forward

  • Upside: A sustained move above the 100-day MA (1.2660) would shift focus to 1.27289, followed by the 50% retracement at 1.2765 and the 200-day MA at 1.2788.
  • Downside: With buyers faltering, sellers hold the short-term edge. Key support lies at 1.26076, with a swing area between 1.2596 – 1.26147 acting as a critical zone to break for further downside momentum. Below that, the next key levels are 1.25499, followed by another swing area at 1.2474 – 1.24999.

For now, GBPUSD remains at a technical crossroads, with the 100-day MA acting as a major barometer into next week’s trading.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1215 ET - Corn futures on the CBOT have had a strong week, but they are leading row crop futures down, amid speculation that U.S. farmers will plant more corn than initially anticipated. "Corn is clearly showing some signs of weakness setting in on several fronts," says Sterling Smith of StoneX. "First ideas about seeing bigger than expected corn acres are keeping the buyers to the sidelines… the solid recent run up is moving grain off the farm which is adding to the pressure." Most-active corn is down 1.1%, while soybeans fall 0.5%. Wheat is up 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1201 ET - Energy futures in general were the chief focus for commodities traders this week, says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. Natural gas and oil futures trended higher — with cold weather globally helping propel gas while oil got a lift from concerns of supply curtailments. According to data compiled by Saxo Bank, energy futures gained over 4% this week, while metals and grains gained under 2% and softs lost roughly 1%. But overall, gains in the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index are outpacing the S&P 500 amid President Trump's tariff push, says Hansen. "The near future points to heightened uncertainty," he says. "So far, commodities have emerged as one of the winners amid increased demand for key raw materials, which could face supply challenges." (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1136 ET - Lean hog futures continue to slide, with the most-active contract down 0.7% in morning trade. If they stay on the downside, then it'll be the third straight day that the continuous contract has finished lower. Lower pork cutout prices have been weighing on futures, this after the CFTC reported a growth in managed money traders holding long positions in hog futures in its Commitment of Traders report last week. This week's move may mean that fund traders are pulling back, says StoneX in a note. "Have we flushed it enough?" the firm asks. "Long liquidation has been quite apparent, though fund positions — which we'll get an update on this afternoon — would still remain on the upper end of historicals." Most-active live cattle is up 0.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.2% at $9,539 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 1.6% at $2,686 a ton. Prices remain elevated however, with copper up 0.85% and aluminum up 1.9% on week. President Trump's tariff policies prompted a squeeze in New York traded copper prices, which saw futures prices spike to a nine-month high last week. This widened the U.S. premium over LME prices to more than $1,000 a ton, Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen says in a note. Since then, prices have started to drift lower with the premium narrowing, though it remains significantly elevated, Hansen writes. Recent copper market actions have been mostly about U.S. commodities exchange Comex buying to preempt tariffs, more than actual end-user demand, Hansen adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1025 ET - Following a 27% devaluation against the dollar in 2024, the Brazilian real is up 8% this year. A report by BNP Paribas says that local funds decreased their BRL short positions to a negative 6.4% on February 17 from a negative 9.3% on January 31. The funds' foreign asset long positioning fell to 2.2% from 6.3%, BNPP says. The bank adds the move coincided with dollar weakness "and the removal of excess premium" after a BRL selloff in December. Over the past year, concerns about increasing government spending have been behind the currency weakness. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0947 ET - Crude futures are lower but on track to end a choppy trading week with gains. "Crude found support this week from an expectation that OPEC+ will delay its production cut once again, in light of crude prices remaining below $80 a barrel," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "The market will digest what OPEC's next move is and if they extend production cuts from April to May, we could see more support, but in the meantime, it looks like WTI is fading," he adds. WTI is off 1.3% at $71.54 a barrel and Brent is down 1.1% at $75.63 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0943 ET - Oil prices are forecast to fall further in the coming months even if OPEC+ decides to delay its planned output hike, according to Capital Economics. The cartel and its allies are set to start raising output from early April, but market watchers widely expect the group to postpone its plans once again amid broad uncertainties around the global economic and geopolitical outlook. Meanwhile, global demand remains subdued despite a slight pick up in the fourth quarter of last year and non-OPEC+ growth is expected to remain robust. "OPEC+'s dilemma isn't set to get any easier this year," says economist Kieran Tompkins. "A further delay would be reflective of weak fundamentals in the oil market." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0910 ET - U.S. natural gas picks up from yesterday's retreat with a widening inventory deficit keeping futures well above $4/mmBtu across the curve. This week's Arctic weather is expected to be reflected in a seventh straight triple-digit storage draw in next week's EIA report, and winter conditions are seen extending into March. "After the warm spell, some predict it will get cold again. This expectation is maintaining the demand for natural gas," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. The Nymex front month is up 2.9% at $4.273/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0855 ET - With potash appearing to make a comeback, Nutrien is positioned to benefit. In a Raymond James report, Steve Hansen says he sees tangible signs of life in the global potash markets, including solid demand and rising prices across most global regions. Nutrien management also sees pricing momentum continuing in 2025, Hansen says, thanks to strong grower affordability as crop prices rise, strong demand from Brazil and Southeast Asia, and balanced inventory levels across key markets such as China, India and Southeast Asia. Hansen says that other key providers in Laos and Russia are currently facing operational challenges and maintenance outages. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0717 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.2% at $9,535.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 1.3% at $2,695 a ton. However, the base metals complex is set to end the week higher on mounting concerns of trade wars and a generally weaker U.S. dollar, with copper up 0.7% on week and aluminum up 2.3% on week. Among North American copper miners, 2024 copper production was on average 5% below original guidance, and 2025 guidance was 5% below consensus estimates, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. This likely reflects more conservatism on guidance from miners and more challenging mining. Some recent positive signs around U.S.-China relations are constructive for metals and the fundamentals for copper look solid, though uncertainty remains short-term, RBC adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Gold futures slip, but remain close to record highs. Futures are down 0.25% at $2,948.70 a troy ounce after a fresh high of $2,973.40/oz in the prior session. The precious metal is set to finish the week higher on safe-haven demand and attention from U.S. President Trump's administration, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Trump insisted he would "make sure the gold is there" when asked about concerns over the Fort Knox gold supply and the rally gained steam after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent mentioned monetizing the U.S. balance sheet, SP Angel says. However, Bessent dismissed revaluing U.S. gold reserves, and ruled out gold as an asset for a new U.S. sovereign wealth fund, SP Angel writes. Consistent central bank purchases and Chinese consumer buying also continue to support gold, SP Angel adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0516 ET - Palm oil ended higher, supported by an improving regional economic landscape and the potential for short-covering activities ahead of the weekend, which could provide a supportive tone to the market, according to Kenanga Futures analysts in a commentary. Looking ahead, the next key resistance is at MYR4,700/ton, according to Phillip Nova analyst Lim Tai An. A successful breakout above this level could propel prices to MYR4,770/ton, while failure to break through would likely lead to a retracement towards the MYR4,550 level, the analyst says. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rose MYR22 to MYR4,664/ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Toronto Stocks Fall; Lundin Gold Shares Rise on 50% Dividend Raise
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks lower higher Friday at midday, ending the week on the back foot.

Most sectors were lower, with heavy losses in the health-services sector. Materials and energy were also laggards. Among the few gainers, the utilities, tech and communication sectors were the leaders.

On the broader economic front, early indications suggest Canadian retail sales fell 0.4% in January, a month after their biggest climb in over two years, with the food-and-drink and automotive sectors leading broad gains.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell 0.6%, to 25368.64, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 retreated by 0.5%, to 1526.14.

Lundin Gold shares rose by 4.3%, to 40.03 Canadian dollars (US$28.24), after the Canadian mining company said it would increase its quarterly dividend by 50%, to 30 U.S. cents.

Other market movers:

Shares of StorageVault Canada rose by 6.3%, to C$3.97, after the company reported better-than-expected revenue and a narrowed loss in the fourth quarter as occupancy trends improved.

Dye & Durham appointed Sid Singh as its interim chief executive, effective immediately, and Arnaud Ajdler as chair, succeeding Hans Gieskes who held both roles following a recent activist investor-led board reshuffle. Shares fell 1.6%, to C$12.15.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Canada Retail Sales Slide After Jumping 2.5% in December — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canadians look to have pared back spending at the start of the year, tightening their belts after flocking to retailers in December and driving up sales at the fastest rate in roughly two and half years.

Sales fell an estimated 0.4% in January, based on Statistics Canada's advance look at retail receipts.

That pullback, despite consumers still benefiting from a temporary federal tax break, may suggest shoppers took a breather and snapped a six-month run of rising sales. Or it could hint at some hesitancy given clouds over Canada's economy and threats of a trade dispute with the U.S.

Statistics Canada offered no details with the estimate for January, which was based on the responses of roughly 58% of retailers surveyed and will be revised.

The retreat in January only partly retraced a stronger-than-expected rise in sales the month before, and a lull was expected by economists after the typically busy Christmas period.

"We'll need to wait to see if the year-end optimism truly faded to start 2025," Bank of Montreal senior economist Shelly Kaushik said.

Data shows goods prices ticked up in January, and Kaushik said retail-sales volumes could still come in roughly flat for the month.

Consumers may have been encouraged to continue spending in the early part of the year by the two-month suspension of the goods-and-services tax on items including children's clothing, toys and restaurant meals, which came to an end mid-February.

Royal Bank of Canada's analysis of anonymized card transactions by Canadian customers showed significantly less was spent in January on clothing and electronics even as grocery baskets got a little lighter. One bright spot in the lender's January data was home-related spending on furniture and household electronics, as well as on building materials.

Tony Stillo, director of Canadian economics at Oxford Economics, said the tax holiday along with recent rebates given out by Ontario's government to households in the country's most populous province are likely to support higher spending in the first quarter of the year, but there are question marks beyond that. "We'll have to wait and see the extent to which heightened uncertainty from Trump's threatened trade war with Canada causes households to curtail spending, especially for big-ticket items," he said.

Sales in December surged 2.5% from the month before to a seasonally adjusted 69.59 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about US$49.09 billion. Compared with a year earlier, retailers saw a 3.9% jump in sales in the last month of 2024.

The performance was much stronger than the 1.6% advance economists expected and the data agency had predicted. There were gains in each of the retail segments tracked by Statistics Canada and in each province, and overall sales volumes also were up a solid 2.5% for the month.

After sales for November were revised higher, it left growth in retail trade in the final quarter of last year accelerating to 2.4% following the weakness in the first two quarters of 2024.

Motor-vehicle and parts dealers were a big driver for the month, with new-car sales up for a third consecutive month. Sales at gas stations and fuel vendors also showed a big increase thanks to higher prices.

Core sales, which strip out car and auto-parts dealers and gas stations, were up 2.5% after weakness in November.

Food-and-beverage retailers also helped drive sales, with strong spending at supermarkets and at beer, wine and liquor stores. Higher receipts were also recorded at general merchandise retailers and clothing, accessories and leather-goods retailers in December.

A string of six interest-rate cuts by the Bank of Canada since June has lowered borrowing costs and helped drive household spending, offsetting weakness in business investment. The federal tax holiday helped keep price pressures in check in January, with annual inflation staying below the central bank's 2% target despite picking up slightly thanks to rising energy costs.

Still, the outlook for Canada's economy has become more uncertain under the looming threat from the White House of hefty tariffs on Canadian exports and President Trump's repeated calls for the country to become the 51st U.S. state. Business surveys indicate companies are more hesitant, with some delaying investment decisions and looking at supply chains in case tariffs hit.

"Consumer spending was clearly improving during the second half of 2024, even before accounting for the temporary boost in December from the GST holiday. However, recent tariff uncertainty may have resulted in households tightening the purse strings again if there was concern regarding employment prospects," Andrew Grantham, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets, said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: StealthGas Shares Rise on Increased Share Buyback Program, 4Q Earnings
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

Shares of StealthGas rose after the company increased the size of its stock repurchase program and logged a jump in profit and revenue for the fourth quarter.

The stock was up 8.1% to $6 in Friday morning trading. Shares are up 5.8% year to date.

The provider of international energy seaborne transportation services to liquefied petroleum gas sectors said its board of directors approved buying back an additional $5 million in company shares to its existing $25 million buyback program.

For the fourth quarter, the company posted a net profit of $14.2 million, or 38 cents a share, compared with $8.9 million, or 25 cents a share, for the same period a year earlier.

Revenue rose 27% to $43.5 million, StealthGas said.

The company is in a solid footing, Chief Executive Harry Vafias said, adding that the company is now net debt free after having further reduced its debt in the current quarter.

"We are optimistic for the future albeit evermore cautiously not least because the current global geopolitics that can have a strong influence on shipping markets are for the time being quite opaque with too many developing situations," Vafias added.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Gasoline, Oil Contract on Course for Week-to-Week Decline — OPIS
data.description:
Petroleum futures were trading sharply lower ahead of midday Friday and are on course to end the day with week-to-week declines as markets react to Trump administration efforts to boost global oil supply.

Crude oil and diesel contracts were off by about 2% as of 11 a.m. ET, while gasoline futures were about 1.8% lower.

ULSD contracts are still slightly above where they settled on Feb. 14, while crude and gasoline are both lower.

The declines follow reports that the White House is pressing Iraq to resume oil exports from the country's Kurdish region. The move is seen as an effort to offset supply losses from expected U.S. sanctions on Iran aimed at choking off oil exports from the country.

The NYMEX April West Texas Intermediate contract was down $1.55 to $70.93/bbl, leaving it 25cts below when it closed out last week. The May WTI contract was $1.53 lower at $70.76/bbl. The lightly traded April Brent contract was off by $1.49 to $74.99/bbl and the more-active May contract was $1.43 lower at $74.62/bbl.

Gasoline futures are more than 5cts below where they settled on Feb. 14, with the March RBOB contract down by 3.91cts to $2.0474/gal. The more-active April contract, which represents the more expensive summer-grade specification, was off by 3.83cts to $2.2834/gal.

The front-month ULSD contract remains about 0.25ct above last week's settlement even as it weakened by 5.36cts to $2.4498/gal. The more-active April contract was down 5.13cts to $2.3953/gal.

The possibility of increased exports from Iraq increases the uncertainty over global oil supply and demand.

These include whether OPEC and its allies will unwind voluntary production cuts later this year, whether a possible peace deal in Ukraine will increase Russian oil exports and the impact of Trump's plan to impose tariffs on goods from multiple U.S. trading partners.

While most of these factors have posed headwinds for prices, continued cold weather across much of the U.S. has helped to support ULSD prices even as oil and gasoline contracts weaken.

data.title: Young traders don't care if stocks and crypto are in a bubble. They could get a rude awakening.
data.description:
By Gordon Gottsegen

A new survey found that the majority of active traders are bullish, despite some thinking the market is in a bubble

Stock-market bulls have been celebrating lately. The S&P 500 SPX has clocked in two consecutive years of more than 20% growth, and is already up around 3.5% in 2025.

The majority of individual traders think the good times will continue throughout the year. However, a significant portion traders also believe that the market is in a bubble.

Although it may seem like being bullish and, at the same time, thinking the market is in a bubble is contradictory, this is the sentiment captured in a recent survey by Nasdaq and Tastytrade. The survey polled 1,036 retail investors who trade stocks at least every three months, and have $1,000 worth of investments or more.

One of the key findings of the survey was that the majority of respondents were bullish about market performance in the next 12 months.

"Fifty-nine percent of active traders are bullish. One in 10 are very bullish. That's a really staggering number for me," Scott Sheridan, the chief executive of Tastytrade, told MarketWatch.

Meanwhile, only 16% of respondents were bearish, and 24% reported feeling neutral.

Survey respondents also wrote down the reasons they were feeling bullish. Some of those reasons included:

  • "I think that the stock market will have record performance this year on the back of AI."
  • "Because we have regulations coming to the crypto market."
  • "I feel good about the strength of the market and believe that there will be more business-friendly policies in the next 12 months."
  • "President Trump will fix the economy."

Almost all (86%) active traders were excited about stocks and equities. And among stocks, most traders (75%) were somewhat or very optimistic about the information-technology sector.

After stocks and equities, crypto was the second-most exciting asset class to traders, but this excitement varied by age. Seventy-seven percent of traders 18 to 34 years old were excited about crypto, compared to 65% of those 35 to 54 and only 31% of those 55 and older.

Despite that excitement, young investors were more likely to believe crypto is in a bubble. Fifty-seven percent of those age 18 to 34 thought crypto was in a bubble, compared to 46% of people age 35 to 54 and 48% of those 55 and older.

Active traders were also asked which kinds of stocks are in a bubble. Artificial-intelligence stocks, social-media stocks, meme stocks and "Magnificent Seven" stocks were common answers, with responses ranging between one-third and one-half of those surveyed, depending on the age group.

On top of that, the youngest cohort was always the most likely to think specific investments are in a bubble.

Recall that young investors were also more excited about equities and crypto, and you may be scratching your head.

"I'm confused because they're diametrically opposed," Sheridan said. "When I look at it, my feeling is it doesn't make sense, other than most people have FOMO."

Sheridan pointed to that fear of missing out as the possible explanation. Traders have been watching - or participating in - the bull market for stocks and crypto, and they want to continue to enjoy the spoils. This bias could explain the general sense of bullishness among the survey respondents.

"It's easy to enjoy the upside. The problem is, what happens if it turns around?" Sheridan said.

Traders may be looking at current valuations for stocks and crypto, and in their heads they may worry about the bubble popping - but that doesn't seem to be influencing their behavior yet.

Why not? Sheridan pointed out that the last real market downturn caused by an economic recession - ignoring the quick pandemic drop in 2020 - was during the Great Recession in 2008. Investors who are 18 to 34 years old are likely too young to remember or have been actively trading during that time. As a result, this creates a gap between what young traders think a bubble is, and what a bubble bursting actually feels like.

So these contradictory sentiments may continue to exist until the next bubble pops.

"We'll have to see how this bull market ends," Sheridan said.

In the meantime, he noted that investors should practice discipline - and stick to their strategy when the market is going up, and when it's going down.

-Gordon Gottsegen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: DAX Index Edge Lower as Germany Heads to the Polls
data.description:
German equities staged a last-minute decline after spending much of the session in the green ahead of its election weekend, with the blue-chip DAX index 0.12% in the red at closing.

The HCOB Flash Germany Composite PMI Output Index rose to 51 in February, the highest level in nine months. The flash reading reflects sustained business activity expansion in the services sector alongside the slower decline in factory output.

"Overall, the economy seems to be back on a growth path, at least for the first two months of the year. According to our GDP Nowcast, which takes the PMI and other data into account, the drag on growth from manufacturing is getting smaller, and the growth in services is making up for it," Hamburg Commercial Bank Chief Economist Cyrus de la Rubia said. "Looking ahead, much of the mood will depend on the capability of the new to be elected government to signal stability and to take bold measures."

In the wider eurozone, the flash composite PMI was unchanged month over month at 50.2, indicating the region's sustained but marginal business activity expansion.

On the corporate side, Volkswagen and other automakers are under investigation by the Italian Competition Authority for a potential violation of the consumer code. The inquiry is focused on information provided to consumers involving electric vehicle driving range, battery capacity and battery warranty limits. The German carmaker was 1.09% higher at closing on Xetra.
